speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five, interview with Selma Engel. You were telling us that some of the survivors wanted to shoot Chaim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. There were-- and then I jumped in front of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You jumped in front of Chaim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Chaim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I saw that-- I know I wouldn't live without Chaim. You know, I love him. And to protect him, I say. I don't know, instinct. Just instinct. I don't know why. I have no-- you didn't think. You do everything by instinct, you know? Instinct, is that English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So, yeah. So, I don't know. Just, you did it. And so they-- then we went away. Chaim and I were the two. And they went by themselves. We don't know where they went. And so we went together. And we walked, and we walked, we walked. And all we walked was three miles away from Sobibor-- three kilometers I think. No, three miles away from Sobibor. Because we went around.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was raining. And we came to a farm, to a house, a little house. And Chaim asked these men, can we stay here overnight? And he say, yes. And Chaim give them-- Chaim had an where you put a glasses, eyeglass holder, formed with diamonds and gold pieces. I have no idea. We thought we were rich. But I don't know how much money that was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave them a gold piece. And we were allowed to stay in the attic. It was our first honeymoon night. And we were-- no, we didn't have honeymoon, believe me. [LAUGHS] We stayed in the attic. And we heard noise outside all the time, of course. It was terrible. It was a terrible. I had to go to the bathroom. I didn't know this man. We didn't know this man, you know. It was horrible. It was a horrible time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a young girl. I was 21 years old. How old was I? 21, yeah. I was just 21 years old. It was just horrible. So we-- what I heard later, that the German came. And they went from house to house. And they didn't came to the house that we stayed. Isn't that a miracle? So they killed almost all the Jews. They came at home. People from Berlin, and from the military, and from all over, they came looking for the people that run away from Sobibor. And they caught many, many, many. And they didn't came to that house. So in the morning, I had to go to the bathroom. I never forgive myself. What can you do? You have to go to the bathroom in the attic. And people were so nice that we could stay there. They were thinking, dirty Jews. Many times I think about it. Isn't that stupid?

speaker: interviewee
text: But, anyway, we run away from there, farther. And we walked later on the stars. We walked on the stars. Chaim wanted to go to the far away was hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: You walked on the stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on the long wagon, the long wagon. Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: You watched the stars.

speaker: interviewee
text: Watched the stars. No, we don't walk on stars. We watched the stars. And at night-- when we walked, when we walked at night. And at daytime, we tried to start hiding somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we asked always people if we could hide somewhere. So we walked, and that was our guide. And we walked at night. And it was very scary.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked in the woods. And I saw a tree. And I saw it was [INAUDIBLE]. They had this big cape they wore. Oh! I was very scary. And he say to me, no, you have to stop Saartje. Scared me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once we went in a very big woods. We went in. There was a body laying on the ground. And we had to step over it. And in the woods you see animals. And you see these green eyes, you know? And you think-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was very scary.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked. And Chaim remembered that we stayed sometimes overnight, by Polish, by Poles. I don't remember. I remember once we stay in a haystack. And came kids outside. And they started running. And they say, oh, we go up at the haystack. And we laying deep in the haystacks. And then we came out. And they say, oh, Jews! Jews!

speaker: interviewee
text: So we jumped out. And we start walking in middle, in daytime. And there was no woods around us. And we start walking there. And there came two people with a wagon and a horse. And they say, you want us to take you somewhere? And Chaim say, yes. So they took us in the wagon. But they didn't know that Chaim spoke Polish. And they say something to each other. I don't what did they say. But they say that they want to kill us. And Chaim say to me, Selma, jump! So we jumped. Because they want to kill us. And they think we have money. And they want to take our money. Because they know about Sobibor. But it was not far from Sobibor. But we were-- how far can we walk? So we walked at night in the woods. And in daytime, we were hiding in the woods or-- yeah, in the woods of-- in fields, or somewhere. But how long could we do that? Because we eat-- we had nothing to eat. He's saying we got some food sometimes from people, but I don't remember. And we stole sometimes carrots from the field, And at night sometimes, we walked at night. And then dog come in back of us, you know? It was very scary. You know, every minute you thought, that's the end. So after 10, 12 days walking-- we don't know exactly how many days and how many nights. We walked. We came by a farmer. And Chaim say to him, can you hide us? And the man say, no. You could stay overnight in the farm, and I will bring you tomorrow morning to my brother. He lives isolated somewhere. And I will bring you to my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we trusted this man. And Chaim was on the wagon, a very old fashioned wagon, which you see in pictures sometimes, from 100 years ago. And he put branches over Chaim. And he dressed me up like a Polish woman. Chaim always say like an old lady, like I am now. But I was more like a Polish woman. And he brought us to his brother. And we came there, in the evening. He let us wash. And he saw how we had scabies over our whole body, and legs, and everything. And he say, you can stay in my-- and he saw also the money that Chaim had. I don't know. But, anyway, he say, you can stay in the barn. And he put us-- the cows were under, and top were pieces of wood. And there was straw laying. And we were allowed to sleep on the straw. And we gave him all the money what we had. And he really-- he saved our life. They-- I have a little diary. I can almost not read it anymore. And I read about it. I complain constantly about it. But these people went through a tremendous danger. And they brought us once a day some food. There was a pail in the front of us, and that was our bathroom. We couldn't walk. And we couldn't talk. I was always [INAUDIBLE]. Somehow Chaim had very little. And, also, the lice went all to me. I had sweeter blood than Chaim. But it went all to me. And I had some kind of-- something-- a disease that was with a [? genital ?] portion in it, and in my-- other people years ago had it in the neck, and had it here and there. I was a mess, a big mess. But anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we stayed there. And they brought us once a day food. And it was very dangerous what this man did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had a little boy, four years old. And one night, once-- different occasions, whatever, we snored. Chaim snored, if I snored, at nighttime when we slept. And there was a woman under there. And she say what is that? He say, I don't know. Thinking some animals also upstairs. So from that time, we were not allowed to sleep in the same time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also they had a stork. It was on the chimney. And they believe when the stork goes away-- a stork is a big bird-- then there is some danger coming, a fire, or danger. And the stork went away. And he say, you have to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he say to us the next day, you know, do you watch the stork? And when we say, yeah. That's all we do. We have nothing else to do. And there was a little hole in the roof straw, from straw roof. And he said, you stop watching it. So thank God, the stork came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I can tell you a hundred anecdotes that-- it was very cold in the winter. And it was very hard from the cows with flies. So we were on them and had wounds all over, all flies. So we lay nude, because we had from everything just one clothes. We had one underpants, and one t-shirt. And some things he burned because he thought to get the scabies and what I had out of-- the lice dies this way. And so we had nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we save that. And then we were laying there nude, and with a piece of something to get the flies out from us. And in the winter, we finally, to give us something to sleep under. And when-- you know, we couldn't move.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so one day, she say to us-- they say to us, that we had to go down and we had to stay with the pigs. So we had to sleep with the pigs together, so they could clean out. But we think they didn't trust us, that we give all the money. Because we know there is some kind of an ointment that you put on scabies. And you put it on, then it heals right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we gave everything. Chaim gives his watch. He has a gold pen. He gives the gold pen. And they still-- they went once buying it. And I read it in my diary. He went once buying it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then we were very afraid that something happened to him, because she was not so nice. And also, I was pregnant. But I didn't know. And years, and years, and years, and years after the war, we never talked about that. That was-- it was not happened. But anyway, I didn't know. Because through the war, a lot of woman didn't have their period. And I didn't have a period. And I was pregnant. And we didn't believe it, of course. And you could see it on my face, of course. And she say, you have to go. You cannot stay here long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was saying that? The woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman. The woman. And we talked her out of it. Well, we didn't believe it ourselves, you know? And I was already three, four, months. I still didn't believe it. I remember my stomach went up and down. And Chaim say, no, it's not true. His stomach went up and down too. But, you know, we didn't believe it. And so, you know, we lived such a-- it was such an-- it was just-- you cannot tell it. But these people saved our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine months. And these people didn't give us much to eat, because they didn't have much for themselves. They were very, very, very poor. And they had nothing. And the house, they made warm with straw on the outside. And they had perhaps a few cows, but they had nothing. And they really saved our life. But I didn't realize in that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam and Stefka Nowak. He died. Chaim thinks he died too. But the little boy for four years old suffered too, the same way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, when-- then also just a few days before the war was over, a little boy was to visiting them, because the German were pulling back. So the people where the German lived, they went to his house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a little bird. And he jumped. And he went to us. And the little boy goes after this little bird. And at once he sees us laying there. So there was all these people that were in the house, with his family, knows about it. So thank God, the next day the German-- you could see the Russian came.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Chaim went down. And we were free. The Russian came. And he went down. And he didn't came up. I think, oh, God. He's gone. He left me all alone. But he came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I go up. And I'm six-months pregnant. I didn't know. I didn't know. Because I didn't have much education what belongs about that subject. And I couldn't walk. My legs were so completely-- we didn't walk in nine months. We didn't talk loud in nine months. And we didn't walk nine months. So I had to learn to walk all over again. And all these people were there. And they went slowly, of course, away. And we were outside. And Chaim helped him in the field. And I was sitting on the grass. And it was beautiful. It was heaven. But the Polish people didn't like it that we were there. And they were planning to kill us. So he brought us--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The farmer told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Nowak told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something going, yeah. They were very hatred that we were there. So we were afraid. So he brought us with the wagon to Chelm. We were next to Chelm. We were hidden. So we wasn't far from Chelm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get clothes now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the same clothes. I had clothes. I had a pair of slacks, you know, that was fashioned [INAUDIBLE] over these pants. Because years ago there was [? the ?] [? older ?] [? women. ?] And had one pair of slacks. And I had a blouse. And underpants. One pair of underpants. And Chaim the same way. That's all we had. So [INAUDIBLE]. We had clothes that we came with from Sobibor, that we had from Sobibor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the farmer brought us to Chelm. I remember he won't go out with the wagon. And when the woman then went a pail of water and there had been tough luck. So he went back. And then the woman was gone. And then we went, brought us to Chelm.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chelm there was one house, from Jewish-- from a Jew. Coincidentally, we met him here in America, where his father owned that house. And there were all the Jews already [INAUDIBLE]. I thought that we were the only Jews who were alive. And I thought every concentration camp was like Sobibor. And I said, all the Jews were killed from Sobibor. But I didn't know there was another camp. And I didn't know about [? partisans, ?] [INAUDIBLE]. You know, you don't think of even. I didn't hear about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to that house. Who told you about the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I don't know. I think the farmer knows perhaps about it, that he brought us there or so. And Chelm was nothing. Chelm was a little street and that's all Chelm. Chelm was 2 houses, 10 houses, very poor. It has a name because of [INAUDIBLE] if you know too many stories about Chelm. But Chelm is nothing. Anyway, my eyes was nothing. And the place was in a house. And all the Jews were there at least in the house, I think. Perhaps were 50. But then I started helping cooking, because we had nothing! Absolutely nothing. So they paid me for that. Although they couldn't eat the food, perhaps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who paid you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish Jews had money. And what did they do a lot? See, it was already now past, almost a week past that the Russians were gone. So they went back to Sobibor, or to other camps. But I told you, Chaim put money in the ground. And they went back to Sobibor. And they tried to get money out of the ground, so much as possible, because that's what-- you know, there's money, gold and diamonds, and everything is there in Sobibor, where Sobibor was, is hidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: So not only the Jews, but the Polish went there too. They were killing each other. I says to Chaim, you don't go back. Are you nuts? We made it so far. You don't go back. We will make it without it. So he didn't go back. So we were only one almost from everybody that had no money. And he had to go in the army, fighting with the Polish army, fighting against the Germans. Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim!

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took him into the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we-- they told him that he has to go up, and he had to go in the army. So he started working as a nurse in a hospital. So, and he as a nurse. He's afraid to kill a fly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- there was a transport from a train accident. And there were people came there, and cutting [INAUDIBLE], legs off. And it was terrible. And he came home, and he said, you don't get me back there. I don't. So in that house, we slept-- Chaim and I, we slept on one person's-- some from the military, something like some bed. And we had no blankets or anything. And on the jacket that Chaim had, was our blanket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the other people slept on the floor. We were fortunate we slept on the beds. We could have better slept on the floor, I think. But, anyway, we slept on-- because I was pregnant. And I helped cooking. And then somehow I had an accident, but I don't remember. That I read in that book that I burnt my leg very severe. And so I couldn't cook anymore. And I start sewing for a woman what survived from Sobibor, and lived in Chelm. And she had clothes. And she lives in-- she's alive. And I started sewing. Her dress is small, so by hand I start sewing. I think that was for her. Perhaps it was for somebody else. I never talked about it with her. And so, yeah-- so that I get a little bit money on. And Chaim somehow-- I don't know what he did. He tried to buy gasoline and sold it something. I don't know. He tried always to make a little bit money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there's a little story that somebody found money in Sobibor at the end of the [INAUDIBLE]. And he had $100 bill. And Chaim knows already a little bit people around from that. He went looking for somehow to make money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he say, perhaps I know somebody. There was a hundred dollar bills, and there was-- the number was gone. And Chaim thought, I don't know, so many years ago how he thought that he could find somebody. We put this $100 bill under our hat from-- and jacket, which we had made for a pillow, or something like that, from our clothes. And so at night, when we were sleeping, I don't know how, we were-- somebody took the hundred dollar bill from the person what we got it from. Somebody stole it. And it was terrible, of course. For they thought we stole it, and we had it, that we were lying. But we were not lying! It was the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when Chaim made some money, he paid that guy off. And now I talk about you, 15 years later, we live in Israel. And we are together. And it was a reunion from people from Sobibor. And we went to Sobibor. Now, first of all-- You went to Sobibor, to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No we went to Sobibor group. But, anyway, in Chelm, Chaim had to pay that guy off, because what we made. And then the guy was there. So I said, do you remember saying that we had this hundred dollar bill under our pillow, and somebody stole it? Oh, yeah. I remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so two days later, he came with somebody else. To break his heart, while we lived in Sobibor. And he said, you know you owe me $100 dollars? So Chaim paid him the $100 dollars. Well, Chaim say, when somebody is so in low, that he will see how difficult we have it, and break his heart at that time, that he needs $100. I won't give him the hundred dollars. Just a story about a hundred dollars.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terrible. We were so poor. We had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was pregnant. And I couldn't walk. My legs were swollen. And I had all the lice. And I had scabies. And then-- it was just-- I really prayed that God will take him to me and that I would be dead. Honest to God. That was terrible. And all the Polish Jews were not very nice to us. And nobody was nice us. Everybody was nice for each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Chaim had to go in military. So what we did, we went away from Chelm and we started traveling. But afraid to hitchhiking on the street, with freight trains, freight cars. And they pulled me then up in the freight car, and we stopped in little villages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think right away we found out there was a Jew. And they took us in. And once we met a guy what was from Sobibor. And I say to Chaim, you know what you do, grow a mustache, change your name, and change that you're older, and then you don't have to go military. So that's what we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, you know what? I lived in a village, Parczew was the name. Yeah, Parczew. And I live in a little villages, one street. And you-- we will find two-- perhaps there's a rich Jewish woman that owns a lot of houses. Don't call houses, what you call here houses. Shacks. You know, nothing. And you can get perhaps there a room. And that's what we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Parczew. And the woman had a little grocery store. And she says, yeah. I have the-- a downstairs, an upstairs. We could go up the stairs, upstairs. And two empty rooms, and the windows were the holes in it. You could-- the wind blows, everything blows through. It was already-- what is it already-- it was-- that was already cold. That'll be cold. And the baby was born in October. And I was pregnant in that time. And went to-- since she gave us these two rooms. It was already fantastic. We had two rooms.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now Chaim started going to old houses, which was wood. And he built a bed for us. And he built a little-- to sit on. When you sat on it, it fell over. He didn't know how to do it exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he found something else to put a table. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we-- in that time, all the time we slept on the floor. And we could clothes off. Our own clothes was our blanket. And then the baby was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the baby born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Parczew. And Chaim was sick. He got a cold. And he had pain. And I had pain in my stomach. But they always told me, when you get a baby, you have pain in your back when the baby was born. I had pain in my stomach. So I didn't think it was the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, we say-- we thought we better go and walk. There was a Red Cross, there was a school. And the Red Cross was there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in between that, Chaim start going traveling. We need money. So Chaim traveled where the Germans moved away, and bought stuff-- shoelaces, and things like that what is not where we lived. And he brought it back and sold it. Don't worry if I brought it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he traveled by-- he jumped on freight trains, at night, far away. He didn't have a warm coat. It was hell for him to. And I was home. So he made a little bit money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, anyway, we walked five miles, or five kilometer-- and I had pain in my stomach-- to that place. And I supposed to get in labor. And it took me five days and five nights, I was in labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was doctors that were for the Germans. That was a Red Cross thing. And they were still there. And were Polish women there. And they told me what to do. And they told me, that I had-- in Polish-- that I had to push. So I pushed. And the baby didn't came out, because it was the six month laying flat. It was three months later, not that it happened-- laying flat. And so, you know, they-- And then the German doctors didn't want to touch me when I had that sickness, that scabies. And they say to me, if you don't stop screaming, we'll throw you out of the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, one of the women, in Polish, say, the head is already out. So then when somebody came, and brought me on the table. And then the baby was born. And then I had to walk to the table, back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then I was laying there [INAUDIBLE] nothing. Chaim still didn't know there was a baby born. So after three days, I think, he found out. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the doctors didn't want to touch me, because I had that very, very contagious skin disease. And they looked all the time under the bed if I was breathing. Then there would be something wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, after five days and five nights, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Chaim came and picked me up with a horse and carriage, and brought me to that apartment there. And then I didn't know what to do with a baby. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made a clothes for my-- Chaim bought already some underpants and things like that. And he bought once a dress. So I made my own clothes for this baby, by hand. I sewed it by hand. I had no idea what clothes baby-- but I had clothes for the baby. And the baby was-- I made a bed, some pillow for something that slept in. So when we came home, the baby slept between us. Chaim built a bed, and the baby slept between us. It was so cold there. So we didn't have-- the wind came over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of the Polish Jews who lived there gives us a-- but got something back, perhaps-- give us a feather bed. But in this feather bed were fleas. And then at night went to bed, these fleas come out. Oh!

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- it were [INAUDIBLE]. He didn't have the problem. I had the problem. The fleas came all to me. So finally, we got also a bed. Chaim made a bed. And now we got also a little feather bed for the baby, or something like that, finally.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then Chaim started traveling far away for five, six weeks at a time to places where the Germans pull away, and to buy some soils, buy some materials, to make a living. How can we make a living? We've come to the end of this tape.
